name: Run Commands in Parallel with xargs and GNU Parallel
description: Speed up batch operations by executing commands in parallel using xargs -P, GNU parallel, and background jobs with wait.

steps.title: Run commands in parallel with xargs
steps.description: Use xargs -P to run multiple processes simultaneously.
steps.tip: -P 4 runs 4 processes in parallel. -print0 and -0 handle filenames with spaces safely.

steps.title: Process a list of URLs in parallel
steps.description: Download or check multiple URLs concurrently using xargs.

steps.title: Use GNU parallel for advanced parallel execution
steps.description: GNU parallel provides job logging, progress bars, and automatic load balancing.
steps.tip: Install with: brew install parallel (macOS) or apt install parallel (Ubuntu).

steps.title: Use background jobs with wait
steps.description: Run commands as background jobs and wait for all to complete.
steps.command:
for i in 1 2 3 4; do
  (echo "Job $i starting" && sleep $((RANDOM % 5)) && echo "Job $i done") &
done
wait
echo "All jobs complete"
steps.warning: Without 'wait', the script may exit before background jobs finish. Always call wait before using the results.

steps.title: Limit concurrency with a semaphore pattern
steps.description: Control the maximum number of concurrent background jobs.
steps.command:
MAX_JOBS=4
for file in *.log; do
  while [[ $(jobs -r | wc -l) -ge $MAX_JOBS ]]; do sleep 0.1; done
  process_file "$file" &
done
wait

fullScript:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Parallel Execution Examples
MAX_PARALLEL=${1:-4}
ACTION=${2:-echo}

log() { echo "[$(date +'%H:%M:%S')] $*"; }

# Method 1: xargs -P
log "=== xargs parallel (max $MAX_PARALLEL) ==="
find . -maxdepth 1 -name '*.txt' -print0 | xargs -0 -P "$MAX_PARALLEL" -I {} sh -c '
  echo "Processing: {}"
  wc -l "{}"
'

# Method 2: Background jobs with wait
log "=== Background jobs ==="
PIDS=()
for i in $(seq 1 "$MAX_PARALLEL"); do
  (
    log "Job $i started"
    sleep 1
    log "Job $i completed"
  ) &
  PIDS+=($!)
done

# Wait and check exit codes
FAILED=0
for pid in "${PIDS[@]}"; do
  wait "$pid" || ((FAILED++))
done

log "All jobs complete. Failures: $FAILED"
[[ $FAILED -gt 0 ]] && exit 1 || exit 0

faq.question: What is the difference between xargs -P and GNU parallel?
faq.answer: xargs -P is built-in and handles simple parallel execution. GNU parallel adds features like progress bars (--bar), job logging (--joblog), retry on failure (--retries), remote execution (--sshlogin), and automatic output ordering. Use xargs for simple cases, parallel for complex workflows.

faq.question: How many parallel jobs should I use?
faq.answer: For CPU-bound tasks, use the number of CPU cores: nproc or sysctl -n hw.ncpu. For I/O-bound tasks (network, disk), you can use 2-4x the core count. Start conservative and increase until you see diminishing returns.

faq.question: How do I handle errors in parallel execution?
faq.answer: With xargs, check the exit code ($?). With background jobs, store PIDs and check each with 'wait $pid'. GNU parallel has --halt now,fail=1 to stop on the first failure or --joblog file.log to record successes and failures.
